text.title: API Versioning Antipatterns
text.summary: Copy-paste handlers, missing headers, CDN Vary bugs, query-param versioning, and agent-specific pitfalls.

antipattern.description: Creating v2 by copying the entire v1 handler file and editing the copy.
antipattern.reason: Both copies diverge immediately. Bug fixes applied to v2 never reach v1. Shared behavior must live in a service layer, with handlers only handling version-specific formatting.

antipattern.description: Introducing a new API version without adding Deprecation, Sunset, and Link headers to old version responses.
antipattern.reason: Without machine-readable deprecation signals, client developers only learn about sunsets when their integration breaks. This creates support emergencies.

antipattern.description: Using ?v=1 or ?version=2 query parameters for public API versioning.
antipattern.reason: Query parameters invalidate CDN cache keys, cause logging blind spots, and are easily missed in SDK generation. Reserve query params for feature flags, not versions.

antipattern.description: Using header-based versioning (Accept-Version) without adding Vary: Accept-Version to responses.
antipattern.reason: CDNs cache based on URL by default. Without Vary, v1 and v2 responses for the same URL are served interchangeably, causing cross-version response contamination.

antipattern.description: Creating a new API version when the change is purely additive (new optional field, new endpoint).
antipattern.reason: Version bumps consume the support window budget. Additive changes are backward-compatible by definition. Signal them via OpenAPI changelog, not a new version.

antipattern.description: Scheduling API version sunset based on time elapsed rather than measured consumer usage.
antipattern.reason: Consumers who call an API infrequently (monthly billing runs, annual reports) are invisible in daily traffic but break catastrophically on sunset. Require traffic-data approval for any sunset decision.
